ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of (a) synthesis of water-soluble N and S-doped CQDs, (b) SiNWs synthesis via MACE, (c) Device fabrication via drop cast method. FESEM images of (d) SiNWs, (e) SiNWs/S1, (f)SiNWs/S2. (g) Schematic representation of fabricated devices.TEM images of (h) SiNWs, (i) SiNWs/S1, (j) SiNWs/S2.

Current (I)-Voltage (V) characteristics of (a) SiNWs/S1, (b) SiNWs/S2 at room temperature and with ON/OFF ratio at V = -1.0 V of (c) SiNWs/S1, (d) SiNWs/S2. Current (I)-Voltage (V) characteristics of (e) SiNWs/S1, (f) SiNWs/S2 with temperature variation.

(a) Comparison graph of
η
-values calculated by the Cheung method of both devices. (b) The dRS/dT vs. T graph of both devices. The [(1/
η
)-1] vs. (1/2kT) and
ϕ B
vs. (1/2kT) graph of (c) SiNWs/S1, (d) SiNWs/S2 device.

ILight to IDark ratio of both devices in (a) reverse bias voltage and (b) forward bias voltage. Comparison of (c) Detectivity and (d) Responsivity with previously developed devices. Plot of Log(I) vs. Log(V) plot for (e) SiNWs/S1 and (f) SiNWs/S2 device.

head.ce:title: Exploring the interfacial behavior of SiNWs/Sulphur and Nitrogen-doped carbon dot based heterostructure for near-infrared photodetection application

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Nanotechnology, Jadavpur University, Kolkata, West Bengal 700032, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Nanotechnology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Nanotechnology, Jadavpur University, Kolkata, West Bengal 700032, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Jadavpur University, Kolkata, West Bengal 700032, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, Jadavpur University, Kolkata, West Bengal 700032, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Table of Content: Photo-response of fabricated devices under Near Infrared (NIR) illumination and Current-Voltage behaviours at low-temperature in dark conditions.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Near-infrared (NIR) photodetectors have been utilized for thermal imaging, surveillance, medical application, and optoelectronics devices with lower energy [1–3]. Photodetectors (PDs) use photon-induced electron-hole pairs at p-n diode junctions to transform NIR light into electrical signals [4]. The indirect band gap topology of bulk Si-based PDs limits light absorption [5]. SiNWs excel in charge transfer, light trapping, anti-reflectivity, and carrier recombination. [3–6]. In contrast, CQDs are often easily and accurately functionalized with foreign atoms, which can modify the band gap and surface charge [7]. So far, the role of surface charge and inhomogeneity between various doped CQDs with SiNWs on NIR photodetector still needs to be improved [8]. In this work, heterostructures of silicon nanowires with sulphur and nitrogen doping in carbon dots were made to overcome interface barriers and achieve high responsivity and detectivity in near-infrared photodetectors. Sulphur-doped CQDs and SiNW heterostructures (p-n junction) showed better detectivity than nitrogen-doped CQDs under IR radiation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of sulphur and nitrogen doped CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Sulphur doped CQDs (S1) and nitrogen doped CQDs (S2) were prepared by simple hydrothermal method (160 °C for 6 h and 4 h) (Fig. 1 a), using sodium citrate (Na3C6H5O7) with sodium thiosulphate (Na2S2O3), arginine (C6H14N4O2) as sulphur and nitrogen donar, respectively.

Vertically grown silicon nanowire was synthesized (Fig. 1b) from an n-Si wafer by metal (Ag) assisted chemical etching (MACE) method [8]. The devices (SiNWs/S1, SiNWs/S2) were fabricated via drop-casting (Fig. 1c). (Detailed in the supplementary experimental section).

Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images of S-CQDs (S1) and N-CQDs (S2) in Fig. S1 (supplementary) appear quasi-spherical with mean particle sizes of 3.3 ± 0.78 nm and 3.03 ± 0.84 nm, respectively. From UV–visible absorption spectra, the indirect band gaps of S1 and S2 were calculated as
E g
= 3.36
eV
and 3.29
eV
, respectively (Supplementary Fig. S2) [6]. Photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra for S1 and S2 show emission maxima (
λ
em) at 436 ± 0.2 nm and 437 ± 0.25 nm under the excitation wavelengths of 350 nm and 370 nm, respectively (Supplementary Fig. S3). Field emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM) and TEM images of SiNWs (Fig. 1d, h), SiNWs/S1 (Fig. 1e, i), and SiNWs/S2 (Fig. 1f, j) showed the vertical structure of synthesized SiNWs, which has a length of 2–6 µm where CQDs were anchored to the top of the SiNWs, respectively. Energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectra and elemental mapping images for SiNWs/S1 and SiNWs/S2 confirm the presence of sulphur and nitrogen in CQDs in devices (Supplementary Figs. S5-6). From UV–visible diffusive reflectance spectra (DRS), the reflectance gradually decreases from 200 to 1100 nm for Si-wafer, SiNWs, SiNWs/S1, and SiNWs/S2, shown in Fig. S4(a). FTIR spectroscopy (Fig. S4(b)) shows the presence of SiO2, Si-Hx, Si-O-Si, C-H, C = O, C = C, CN, C-H, and –OH bonds. The I-V measurements of devices at room temperature (Fig. 2 a,b) under dark and NIR illuminations are evaluated, followed by the thermionic emission (TE) [9] diode equation (S1) (details in the supplementary), and the I-V data is recorded in low-temperature variation (Fig. 2e,f) also. From equation (S2), the ideality factor increases when the temperature decreases from 303 K to 250 K (Fig. 3 a, S9).

Generally, surface defects, high densities of interfacial states, and non-homogeneous doping are the reasons for series resistance (Rs), inhomogeneity in barrier height (
ϕ B
), and differences in ideality factor (η) during temperature variation [10]. This situation demonstrates that electron transfer decreases as the value of η increases with lowering temperature [9]. However, inhomogeneity in barrier height and series resistance are essential explanations for high ideality values [11]. According to the Cheung Method’s
ϕ B
, η, Rs values were evaluated using equations S4–S7 (current–voltage section and Tables A, B, and C in the supplementary) from low-temperature current–voltage [9,11]. Both methods show barrier height increasing with temperature (Fig. S8). Fig. S9 shows that Cheung methods provide relatively lower η values than TE approaches owing to series resistance effects, potential barrier inhomogeneity, and interface states [12]. Fig. S8 shows that Rs values fall for both devices as free carrier density increases at high temperatures [13], and an anomaly is observed at low temperatures. The rate of change of series resistance with temperature (Fig. 3b) is nearly linear in the 303-280 K range while the SiNWs/S2 device has unexpected resistance below 270 K compared to the SiNWs/S1 device. Werner and Guttler suggest that substantial barrier height (
ϕ
BO
) may be found in barrier height distribution (eq. S8) based on temperatures [9,14].

Eqs. S8–S9 (supplementary) were used to calculate the standard deviation (σ) of
ϕ B
distribution, average barrier height
(
ϕ
BO
)
at V = 0, and voltage coefficient of
η
from experimental investigations. From the plot (Fig. 3c, d), [(1/
η
)-1] vs.1/2kT and ϕ vs.1/2kT for both devices identify the standard deviations (σ ∼ 0.312 and 0.306) nearly identical and show comparatively high voltage coefficient (
ρ 2
,
ρ 3
) in SiNWs/S2 device. As a result, the SiNWs/S2 device seems to have more barrier inhomogeneity than the SiNWs/S1 device because inhomogeneity is co-related to the voltage coefficient [15]. For I-V characteristics at room temperature under NIR irradiation (λ = 900 nm wavelength with intensity 8 μW/cm2) for devices. Responsivity (R) and Detectivity (D) in the reverse bias of devices were calculated by equations;
R =
I p
-
I d
P
in
and
D =
A
1 2
R
2 q
I d
1 2
Where
I p
,
I d
,
P
in
represents photocurrent, dark current, and power of incident light in the effective area (A = 0.18 cm2) of devices, respectively [4]. In reverse bias voltages (Fig. 4 a), SiNWs/S1 have more excellent ILight/IDark ratios than SiNWs/S2.

This is mainly due to more p-type character of CQD by sulphur doping than n-type N-CQD [16,17]. The semiconducting nature of our samples was revealed from the slope of the Mott–Schottky plot (Fig. S11) (details provided in supplementary) [18]. From the band diagram, the difference in the position of the valance band maximum (ΔEVBM) of SiNWs/S1 (0.88 eV) is considerable smaller than SiNWs/S2 (1.17 eV) (Scheme S1). Consequently, the SiNWs/S1 heterostructure exhibits a higher level of responsivity and detectivity than the SiNWs/S2 heterostructure (shown in Table D, supplementary). Specifically, at a reverse bias voltage of −1.0 V, SiNWs/S1 shows a responsivity of 622.22 mA/W and a detectivity of 2.15 × 1012 Jones, whereas SiNWs/S2 shows a responsivity of 340.97 mA/W and detectivity of 7.38 × 1011 Jones. A detailed comparison of the device's performance improvement with respect to other devices has been depicted in Table F and Fig. 4c, d.

In Fig. 4(e, f) shows the device Log(I) vs. Log(V) graph distinct into three different regions (region I, II, and III; see Table E in the supplemental). Region I exhibit linear (ohmic) voltage-current behavior [19]. Space-charge-limited current (SCLC) increases current exponentially (I ∼ Vm) in Region II (0.08 < V < 0.74) [20]. The plot slope decreases in Region III (0.94 < V < 1.99) as devices approach trap-filling [21].

In summary, we have incorporated electron donating and withdrawing effects on carbon dots via nitrogen and sulphur doping, enhancing NIR photodetection. Sulphur’s electron-withdrawing effect on CQDs surface increases their p-type character. It overcomes the surface inhomogeneity barrier than that of N-CQD/SiNW heterostructure, improving excellent responsivity and detectivity, making it a promising low-cost alternative for highly efficient NIR-sensitive applications. These findings illuminate SiNWs and doped CQD NIR photodetector design, indicating applications in optical sensing, imaging, and biological diagnostics.

S. Najes Riaz: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Dipayan Roy: Data curation, Formal analysis, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Souvik Bhattacharjee: Data curation. Ankita Chandra: Data curation. Kalyan Kumar Chattopadhyay: Supervision, Conceptualization. Sourav Sarkar: Investigation, Supervision, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors want to thank the Science and Engineering Research Board (SERB), a statutory body of the Department of Science and Technology (DST), Government of India (File No.CRG/2021/002823) for providing financial support. The authors, DR and SB would like to acknowledge the University Grants Commission (UGC), Government of India (Ref No. 191620010342) and the Council of Scientific and Industrial Research (CSIR), Government of India (File No. 09/096(0946/2018-EMR-I) for providing financial support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matlet.2023.134458.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
